name: Torre de Belém from Rua Augusta — route in Lisboa
name_original: Torre de Belém a partir da Rua Augusta — percurso em Lisboa
region_intro: Hiking around Santa Justa, located near Valongo, Portugal, offers diverse trails within the Serras do Porto Park, a classified Protected Landscape. The region is characterized by significant geological heritage, featuring distinctive quartzite ridges and picturesque river valleys. With a maximum altitude of 374 meters, the terrain provides varied elevation for rewarding climbs and expansive panoramic views. This area is recognized for its ecological importance and is part of the Natura 2000 network. Best hiking trails around Santa Justa The most popular hiking route is Torre de Belém from Rua Augusta, a 11.9 miles (19.2 km) trail that takes 4 hours 55 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the Fragas do Castelo. Another top favourite among local hikers is View from the Rua Augusta Arch – Miradouro da Senhora do Monte loop from Glória - Restauradores, a moderate 5.2 miles (8.4 km) path. This trail provides expansive panoramic views over Greater Porto and the distant coastline. Local hikers also love the Restauradores Square – Glória Funicular loop from Restauradores, a 3.2 miles (5.2 km) trail leading through historical mining areas, often completed in about 1 hour 25 minutes. Hiking around Santa Justa is defined by its distinctive quartzite ridges, picturesque river valleys, and panoramic viewpoints. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging paths. The routes in Santa Justa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 1500 reviews. More than 18000 hikers have used komoot to explore Santa Justa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Santa Justa?
A: Santa Justa offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 29 trails to explore. These range from easy strolls to more challenging paths, ensuring there's something for every hiker.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on the trails in Santa Justa?
A: The terrain in Santa Justa is characterized by its significant geological heritage, featuring distinctive quartzite ridges and picturesque river valleys. You'll find varied elevations, with the Serra de Santa Justa reaching a maximum altitude of 374 meters, providing rewarding climbs and diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Santa Justa?
A: Yes, Santa Justa has a good selection of trails suitable for families. Out of the 29 available routes, 20 are classified as easy, making them ideal for hikers of all ages and experience levels. These easier paths often feature gentle slopes and scenic views.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Santa Justa?
A: While specific regulations for dogs on trails can vary, the natural environment of Serra de Santa Justa, as part of the Protected Landscape of Serras do Porto Park, generally welcomes responsible dog owners. It's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash and clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

Q: What kind of views can I expect while hiking in Santa Justa?
A: Hikers in Santa Justa can enjoy expansive panoramic views from elevated points. These vistas often stretch over Greater Porto and the distant coastline. You'll also get charming views of the Ferreira River valley and the village of Couce. The Pico da Serra de Santa Justa loop is particularly noted for its stunning views.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks to see along the trails?
A: Yes, Santa Justa has a rich history, particularly related to mining. The Fojo de Mina – Old Mine Shaft (Fojo de Mina) loop, for example, leads through historical mining areas, offering a glimpse into the region's past. The area also boasts a vast biological, geological, and paleontological patrimony.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Santa Justa?
A: The Serra de Santa Justa offers pleasant hiking conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most comfortable temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late afternoon hikes are recommended. Winter offers a different charm, though some trails might be wetter.

Q: Are there many circular hiking routes in Santa Justa?
A: Many of the popular trails in Santa Justa are designed as loops, offering a complete experience without retracing your steps. Examples include the Couce Bridge – View of Fragas do Castelo loop and the Pico da Serra de Santa Justa – View of Fragas do Castelo loop, allowing you to start and end at the same point.

Q: What makes Santa Justa ecologically important?
A: The Serra de Santa Justa is recognized for its significant ecological value and is part of the Natura 2000 network. This designation highlights its preserved natural environment and the importance of its biodiversity, making it a crucial area for conservation.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Santa Justa?
A: The trails in Santa Justa are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.2 stars from over 160 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, the well-maintained paths, and the rewarding panoramic views.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: While Santa Justa offers many easy and moderate options, there is at least one trail classified as difficult for those seeking a greater challenge. These routes typically feature steeper ascents, longer distances, and more rugged terrain, providing a rewarding experience for seasoned hikers.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the hiking areas in Santa Justa?
A: Santa Justa is located near Valongo, and while specific public transport routes directly to trailheads may vary, Valongo itself is accessible by public transport. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or consider a short taxi ride from Valongo to reach your desired starting point for hikes within the Serra de Santa Justa.
tips: In the center of Praça da Figueira, there is this beautiful and slender bronze equestrian statue of King D. Joao I on a high stone pedestal. It was built in 1971 on the basis of where the old Figueira market was located.

The Arch of Rua Augusta is the unmistakable entrance to the Plaza del Comercio from the street that bears the same name - the main artery of the Baixa - and had been closed for some time due to remodeling works in which the Lisbon Tourism Association was he has spent almost a million Euros.

Praça do Comércio (Commerce Square, better known as Terreiro do Paço), was the land where the Royal Palace of Lisbon sat for more than 200 years. It is one of the most important squares in Lisbon and, with a great view of the Tagus estuary, it is the nerve center of the city.
The 25 de Abril Bridge is a large suspension bridge in Portugal that crosses the estuary of the Tagus River, in the metropolitan area of Lisbon.
The bridge with a view of Christ the Redeemer is just one highlight of many that can be discovered on the riverbank between Belém and Lisbon city center. The best way is by bike.

The Monument to the Discoveries, popularly known as Padrão dos Descobrimentos, is a monument built in 1960, on the banks of the Tagus River, in Belém, Lisbon, to commemorate the 500th anniversary of the death of Henry the Navigator.
This is a comparatively small pseudo lighthouse between the Belm Tower and the Monument to the Discoveries.

The Torre de Belem is the most characteristic building in Lisbon, being the monument in Portugal that comes closest to instant recognition. This delightfully tiny fortress has guarded the Tagus Estuary since the 16th century and was built in the heyday of the Manueline style of architecture.

The Fort of Bom Sucesso is located slightly to the west of the Tower of Belém in Belém in the municipality of Lisbon, in Portugal. It currently houses the Museu do Combatente and the Monument to the Overseas Combatants.

Built in 1940 for the Exposition of the Portuguese World, it is one of the largest and most beautiful public spaces in Lisbon. It serves as a point of union between the Jerónimos Monastery and the Tagus, allowing the beauty of the monument to be enhanced. It stands out for its manicured hedges and monumental fountains that are illuminated at dusk, creating an attractive spectacle and offering a good excuse to return to this area of Belém at night.
The Navy Museum in Lisbon, also known as Museu de Marinha, is an emblematic place that immerses us in the rich Portuguese maritime history, especially during the Age of Discoveries.
Built for the great exhibition of the Portuguese world in 1940, it is characterized by its large lawn areas.
